books.title: Player Piano
books.synopsis: In a near-future America run entirely by machines, engineer Paul Proteus grows disillusioned with a society that has made human labor obsolete. As the gap between the technocratic elite and the displaced masses widens, Paul is drawn toward a revolutionary movement — only to discover that rebellion offers no easy answers either.

books.title: The Sirens of Titan
books.synopsis: Malachi Constant, the luckiest man in America, is swept up in an absurd cosmic adventure that takes him from Earth to Mars to Mercury and finally to Titan. Spanning millennia and galaxies, the novel asks whether free will exists — and concludes that the purpose of human history may be distressingly trivial.

books.title: Mother Night
books.synopsis: American playwright Howard W. Campbell Jr. recounts his wartime life as a Nazi propagandist who was secretly an Allied spy. Now awaiting trial in Israel, Campbell confronts the novel's central moral: we are what we pretend to be, so we must be careful what we pretend to be.

books.title: Cat's Cradle
books.synopsis: A writer researching the day the atomic bomb was dropped stumbles onto ice-nine — a substance capable of freezing all water on Earth — and the eccentric family of its inventor. Along the way he discovers Bokononism, a religion built entirely on harmless lies, and watches civilization unravel anyway.

books.title: God Bless You, Mr. Rosewater
books.synopsis: Eliot Rosewater, heir to a vast fortune, abandons high society to give money indiscriminately to the poor of a depressed Indiana town. As lawyers scheme to have him declared insane and seize the Rosewater fortune, the novel asks what it would truly mean to love all of humanity — especially the useless and forgotten.

books.title: Slaughterhouse-Five
books.synopsis: Billy Pilgrim has come unstuck in time. Moving without control between his WWII imprisonment in Dresden — where he survives the Allied firebombing in an underground slaughterhouse — his postwar suburban life, and his abduction by the alien Tralfamadorians, Billy's story is Vonnegut's anti-war masterpiece. So it goes.

books.title: Breakfast of Champions
books.synopsis: Dwayne Hoover, a Pontiac dealer on the verge of a breakdown, becomes convinced that a Kilgore Trout science fiction story is literal truth — and that he alone is a free human being in a world of robots. Vonnegut enters the novel himself, confronting his own characters and examining the damage stories can do.

books.title: Slapstick
books.synopsis: The last President of the United States, the hideous Dr. Wilbur Daffodil-11 Swain, writes his memoirs from the ruins of Manhattan. He recalls his childhood with his brilliant twin sister Eliza, their scheme to combat loneliness by giving every American thousands of new relatives, and a world where gravity has become unpredictable.

books.title: Jailbird
books.synopsis: Walter F. Starbuck, a minor figure in the Watergate scandal, is released from prison and wanders a corporate America secretly owned by one vast conglomerate. A meditation on labor movements, political idealism, and the grinding disappointment of American life, told by a man who has outlasted every cause he ever believed in.

books.title: Deadeye Dick
books.synopsis: Rudy Waltz accidentally kills a pregnant woman with a rifle shot as a boy and spends the rest of his life atoning for the accident that defined him. Set in the fictional Midland City, the novel ends with a neutron bomb wiping out the entire population — an act of cosmic indifference that feels almost merciful.

books.title: Galápagos
books.synopsis: A ghost narrates the story of a shipwrecked group of tourists who become the sole survivors of a plague that sterilizes humanity. Stranded on the Galápagos Islands, their descendants evolve over a million years into gentle, small-brained creatures. The culprit of all human misery, Vonnegut argues, was the oversized human brain.

books.title: Bluebeard
books.synopsis: Rabo Karabekian, Abstract Expressionist painter and Armenian genocide survivor, is coaxed by an uninvited houseguest into writing his memoirs. Behind the locked potato barn on his estate lies a secret canvas thirty feet tall — a painting that turns out to be his true life's work and a monument to forgotten suffering.

books.title: Hocus Pocus
books.synopsis: Eugene Debs Hartke, Vietnam veteran and former college professor, writes his memoir on scraps of paper while awaiting trial for supposedly masterminding a prison break. A bitter survey of American failure — in Vietnam, in race relations, in education — told by a man who feels personally responsible for every war he fought in.

books.title: Timequake
books.synopsis: The universe suffers a timequake in 2001, forcing every human being to relive the decade from 1991 to 2001 in exact detail, with no free will whatsoever. When free will resumes, humanity is paralyzed — unable to act independently — until Kilgore Trout rallies people with the cry: 'You were sick, but now you're well, and there's work to do.'

characters.name: Kilgore Trout
characters.description: A prolific but largely unread science fiction writer. Vonnegut's most recurring character, appearing across seven novels.

characters.name: Eliot Rosewater
characters.description: Millionaire philanthropist and president of the Rosewater Foundation, who gives money to anyone who needs it.

characters.name: Howard W. Campbell Jr.
characters.description: An American playwright who becomes a Nazi propagandist while secretly working as a spy for the US government.

characters.name: Billy Pilgrim
characters.description: A WWII veteran and optometrist who has become 'unstuck in time,' experiencing his life in non-linear order.

characters.name: Montana Wildhack
characters.description: A movie star abducted by the Tralfamadorians and placed in the same zoo habitat as Billy Pilgrim.

characters.name: Valencia Merble
characters.description: Billy Pilgrim's devoted wife, who dies of carbon monoxide poisoning while rushing to visit Billy in hospital.

characters.name: Edgar Derby
characters.description: A high school teacher and POW who is executed by firing squad for looting a teapot from the ruins of Dresden.

characters.name: Paul Lazzaro
characters.description: A bitter, vengeful American POW who vows to have Billy Pilgrim killed after the war.

characters.name: Roland Weary
characters.description: An anti-tank gunner who blames Billy Pilgrim for his capture and death, igniting Lazzaro's vendetta.

characters.name: Barbara Pilgrim
characters.description: Billy Pilgrim's daughter, who is exasperated by her father's increasingly strange behavior and public claims about time travel.

characters.name: Dwayne Hoover
characters.description: A wealthy Pontiac dealer in Midland City who is going insane, in part after reading Kilgore Trout's novel.

characters.name: Francine Pefko
characters.description: Dwayne Hoover's secretary and mistress at the Pontiac dealership.

characters.name: Bunny Hoover
characters.description: Dwayne Hoover's gay son, who plays piano at the Holiday Inn lounge and is estranged from his father.

characters.name: Harry LeSabre
characters.description: Dwayne Hoover's sales manager, who hides a secret passion for cross-dressing.

characters.name: Rabo Karabekian
characters.description: An Abstract Expressionist painter of Armenian descent whose controversial career culminates in a vast secret masterpiece hidden in his potato barn.

characters.name: Helga Noth
characters.description: Howard Campbell's beloved German wife, who disappears during the Soviet advance near the end of WWII.

characters.name: Resi Noth
characters.description: Helga's younger sister, who resurfaces after the war impersonating Helga to get close to Howard Campbell.

characters.name: George Kraft
characters.description: Howard Campbell's friendly neighbor and chess partner in New York, who is secretly a Soviet spy named Ivan Yankovy.

characters.name: Frank Wirtanen
characters.description: The American intelligence officer who recruits Howard Campbell as a spy and holds the secret to his innocence.

characters.name: Dr. Lionel Jones
characters.description: A white supremacist dentist and publisher of neo-Nazi materials who idolizes Howard Campbell's wartime broadcasts.

characters.name: Senator Lister Rosewater
characters.description: Eliot Rosewater's conservative, patrician father, a US Senator who is appalled by his son's philanthropy.

characters.name: Sylvia Rosewater
characters.description: Eliot's aristocratic wife, who suffers a nervous breakdown from her husband's erratic generosity.

characters.name: Norman Mushari
characters.description: An ambitious young lawyer scheming to have Eliot declared insane in order to redirect the Rosewater fortune.

characters.name: Fred Rosewater
characters.description: A distant Rosewater cousin in Rhode Island whom Mushari hopes to make the new heir to the fortune.

characters.name: John (Jonah)
characters.description: The narrator, a writer researching what important Americans were doing on the day the atomic bomb was dropped.

characters.name: Felix Hoenikker
characters.description: The absent-minded physicist who co-invented the atomic bomb and secretly created ice-nine, a substance that could freeze all the world's water.

characters.name: Frank Hoenikker
characters.description: Son of ice-nine's inventor, who becomes Minister of Science on San Lorenzo and offers John the presidency.

characters.name: Angela Hoenikker
characters.description: Felix Hoenikker's eldest daughter, who sacrificed her youth caring for her father and carries a chip of ice-nine.

characters.name: Newton Hoenikker
characters.description: Felix Hoenikker's dwarf son, who corresponds with John and unknowingly helps trigger the apocalypse.

characters.name: Mona Aamons Monzano
characters.description: The beautiful adopted daughter of San Lorenzo's dictator, a Bokononist saint and John's love interest.

characters.name: 'Papa' Monzano
characters.description: The dying dictator of San Lorenzo, who uses ice-nine to commit suicide, triggering a global catastrophe.

characters.name: Bokonon
characters.description: The founder of Bokononism, a religion built on 'foma' (comforting lies), officially outlawed but secretly tolerated on San Lorenzo.

characters.name: Julian Castle
characters.description: A former millionaire playboy who renounced his fortune to establish a free jungle hospital on San Lorenzo, cynical but genuinely devoted to the poor.

characters.name: Philip Castle
characters.description: Julian's son, who runs the Casa Mona hotel and wrote the definitive history of San Lorenzo; a devout Bokononist.

characters.name: Malachi Constant
characters.description: The richest man in America, who is drawn into Winston Rumfoord's manipulations and ends up on Mars, Mercury, and Titan.

characters.name: Winston Niles Rumfoord
characters.description: A billionaire who flew his spaceship into a chrono-synclastic infundibulum, becoming a wave phenomenon spread across space.

characters.name: Beatrice Rumfoord
characters.description: Winston's haughty wife, who is manipulated by her husband's machinations into becoming Malachi's mate on Mars.

characters.name: Salo
characters.description: A Tralfamadorian robot stranded on Titan for millennia, waiting for a replacement part—the true cause of all human history.

characters.name: Boaz
characters.description: Malachi's companion soldier on Mars, who discovers he can control harmoniums — creatures that live on vibration — and chooses to remain on Mercury rather than return to Earth.

characters.name: Chrono
characters.description: The son born to Malachi and Beatrice on Mars, who becomes a revered primitive figure on Earth before flying off with migrating birds.

characters.name: Paul Proteus
characters.description: A brilliant engineer and manager who grows disillusioned with the automated society he helps maintain.

characters.name: Anita Proteus
characters.description: Paul's ambitious wife, who married him for his status and becomes increasingly estranged as he rebels.

characters.name: Ed Finnerty
characters.description: Paul's old friend from engineering school, who has quit his prestigious job and become a rebel against the system.

characters.name: Reverend James Lasher
characters.description: A former social scientist turned minister who becomes the ideological mastermind behind the Ghost Shirt Society's rebellion against the automated state.

characters.name: The Shah of Bratpuhr
characters.description: A spiritual leader from a distant nation touring automated America, who sees through its prosperity and refers to its citizens as 'takaru' — slaves.

characters.name: Walter F. Starbuck
characters.description: A Harvard-educated government official who served time for his peripheral role in Watergate.

characters.name: Mary Kathleen O'Looney
characters.description: Walter's college sweetheart who becomes a bag lady — and secretly the controlling shareholder of a massive corporation.

characters.name: Ruth Starbuck
characters.description: Walter's devoted wife, a Holocaust survivor and concentration camp librarian who died while he was in prison, leaving him haunted by guilt.

characters.name: Leland Clewes
characters.description: Walter's old Harvard friend whose career was destroyed when Walter named him as a communist before the HUAC; their paths cross again after both have suffered.

characters.name: Rudy Waltz
characters.description: A pharmacist in Midland City who accidentally killed a pregnant woman with a rifle shot as a child, haunted by guilt.

characters.name: Felix Waltz
characters.description: Rudy's father, an admirer of Hitler and failed artist who befriended him in Vienna, leaving the family with a firearms collection.

characters.name: Celia Hoover
characters.description: Dwayne Hoover's wife, a former drum majorette who becomes addicted to diet pills and dies by suicide, her tragedy woven into the fabric of Midland City.

characters.name: Leon Trout
characters.description: Kilgore Trout's son, a ghost narrator who observes humanity's evolution over one million years from the Galápagos Islands.

characters.name: Mary Hepburn
characters.description: A widowed biology teacher who becomes the unlikely mother of humanity's next evolutionary branch on Santa Rosalia island.

characters.name: Zenji Hiroguchi
characters.description: A Japanese computer genius who created Mandarax, a hand-held translation and information device, killed in a terrorist attack in Ecuador before reaching the Galápagos.

characters.name: Hisako Hiroguchi
characters.description: Zenji's wife, a Kana calligraphy teacher who becomes one of the island's survivors; her daughter Akiko is born with a full coat of fur, the first step in humanity's new evolution.

characters.name: Captain Adolf von Kleist
characters.description: The hapless captain of the Bahía de Darwin, whose sperm — unknown to him — Mary Hepburn uses to inseminate the stranded women, making him the unwitting genetic father of the new human race.

characters.name: James Wait
characters.description: A con man who preys on lonely wealthy women by marrying and robbing them; he books the ill-fated Galápagos cruise under a false name and dies on board.

characters.name: Wilbur Daffodil-11 Swain
characters.description: The last President of the United States, a grotesquely tall man who built a utopian extended-family program and now lives out his days in the ruins of the Empire State Building.

characters.name: Eliza Mellon Swain
characters.description: Wilbur's twin sister, whose combined intellect with Wilbur constituted a single brilliant mind; separated from him by their parents, she dies in an avalanche in the Himalayas.

characters.name: Circe Berman
characters.description: A forceful young widow and bestselling author who moves into Rabo Karabekian's Long Island estate and relentlessly pushes him to write his autobiography and confront his past.

characters.name: Dan Gregory
characters.description: Rabo's first mentor, the most celebrated American illustrator of the early twentieth century, a brilliant craftsman and ardent fascist who was killed fighting for Mussolini's Italy.

characters.name: Terry Kitchen
characters.description: Rabo's closest friend among the Abstract Expressionists, a gifted painter who accidentally killed himself and several others with an industrial paint sprayer.

characters.name: Eugene Debs Hartke
characters.description: A Vietnam veteran and physicist turned college professor at a school for the learning-disabled rich, then warden of a prison across the lake, who is ultimately convicted of inciting a prison break.

characters.name: Monica Pepper
characters.description: Works at the Xanadu writers' retreat and is one of the people Kilgore Trout helps after the timequake's rerun ends.

characters.name: Kurt Vonnegut
characters.description: The author himself, who appears as a character in several of his own novels, blurring the line between fiction and autobiography.

interactions.description: Billy and Montana are placed together in a Tralfamadorian zoo, where they become companions and have a child.
Billy and Valencia are married. She dies rushing to see him in hospital after his plane crash.
Both are American POWs sheltered in Dresden's Slaughterhouse-Five during the firebombing.
Lazzaro blames Billy (at Roland Weary's dying request) for Weary's death and vows to have him killed.
Weary drags Billy through the snow during capture, blaming him when he dies of gangrene in a POW boxcar.
Barbara is exasperated by her father's time-travel claims and tries to manage his increasingly erratic public behavior.
Lazzaro and Weary bond as fellow POWs; Weary's dying words instruct Lazzaro to avenge him against Billy.
Both are American POWs transported together in the same boxcar to Dresden.
Campbell appears in Dresden to recruit American POWs to fight for Germany; Billy witnesses this.
Billy discovers Trout's novels in a Times Square porn shop and becomes a devoted fan; they later meet at Billy's anniversary party.
Billy and Eliot share a ward in a veterans' hospital and bond over Kilgore Trout's science fiction novels.
Eliot introduces Billy to Trout's work while they are both recovering in the veterans' hospital.
Vonnegut inserts himself into the Dresden scenes, briefly appearing as a soldier among the POWs.
Dwayne reads Trout's novel 'Now It Can Be Told' and, taking it literally, attacks several people including Trout himself.
Dwayne and Francine are in a long-term affair; she is devoted to him despite his deteriorating mental state.
Dwayne is estranged from his gay son Bunny, who performs piano nearby but keeps emotional distance from his father.
Harry is Dwayne's loyal sales manager; Dwayne's breakdown puts their professional relationship in crisis.
Celia was Dwayne's wife; her suicide by Drano traumatized the family and deepened Dwayne's slide toward madness.
Trout and Karabekian both attend the Midland City arts festival and interact in the hotel bar.
Vonnegut appears as a character who watches over Trout throughout the novel and finally meets him in the bar, granting him freedom.
Vonnegut observes Dwayne's unraveling throughout the novel as an authorial presence in Midland City.
Both are prominent residents of Midland City whose lives intersect in the shadow of the town's recurring tragedies.
Rudy and Celia Hoover move in the same Midland City circles; her death by suicide is one of the many tragedies that define the town.
Eliot is Trout's most devoted fan and has read everything Trout ever wrote; he summons Trout to Rosewater County.
Eliot and Sylvia are married, but his obsessive philanthropy drives her to a nervous breakdown and eventual separation.
The Senator is horrified by his son's behavior and is torn between familial love and embarrassment at Eliot's philanthropy.
Mushari plots to have Eliot declared insane in order to redirect the Rosewater fortune to a distant relative.
Eliot ultimately acknowledges all the children in Rosewater County as his own, thwarting Mushari's plan to install Fred as heir.
Mushari seeks out Fred as the intended beneficiary of his scheme to break the Rosewater Foundation's trust.
Howard and Helga are deeply in love; he calls their marriage a 'nation of two.' She disappears near the end of the war.
Resi impersonates Helga to get close to Howard; he falls in love with her before discovering she is a Soviet agent.
Howard and Kraft are close friends and chess partners in New York; Kraft is later revealed to be a Soviet spy.
Wirtanen recruits Howard as a spy and is the only person who can confirm Howard's innocence — but declines to testify.
Jones idolizes Howard as a hero of white supremacy, oblivious to the fact that Howard's broadcasts were a cover for Allied espionage.
Kraft and Resi are both Soviet agents working together in New York, using Howard Campbell for their own ends.
John's entire investigation begins with Felix — researching the bomb scientist's life reveals the existence of ice-nine and eventually brings John to San Lorenzo.
John travels to San Lorenzo and finds Frank, who offers him the presidency of the island in exchange for marrying Mona.
John interviews Angela as part of his research into her father Felix's life on the day of the atomic bomb.
Newt corresponds with John about his father Felix and later meets him in San Lorenzo.
John falls in love with Mona and agrees to become San Lorenzo's president partly to be with her.
John meets 'Papa' on his deathbed; Papa uses ice-nine to commit suicide, causing worldwide catastrophe.
John visits Julian's jungle hospital on San Lorenzo, where the cynical philanthropist gives him a sardonic tour of suffering and charity.
John meets Philip at the Casa Mona hotel; Philip's hand-written history of San Lorenzo becomes one of John's key sources.
Julian and his son Philip are estranged in temperament — Julian pities the poor while Philip aestheticizes their suffering — but both are devoted Bokononists.
Felix's neglect shaped Frank entirely; Frank idolized his brilliant, oblivious father and inherited a chip of the ice-nine that could end the world.
Angela devoted her childhood to caring for her helpless, brilliant father, surrendering her own life to his; she too carried a fragment of ice-nine.
Felix barely acknowledged Newt's existence; on the morning he showed Newt cat's cradle, he was already thinking about the substance that would doom the world.
Frank gave Papa a chip of ice-nine in exchange for his ministerial position and Mona's hand for John.
Frank was engaged to Mona before offering her to John as part of the presidential deal.
Bokonon and Papa co-founded the religion of Bokononism and its theatrical illegality to give the people of San Lorenzo meaning.
Rumfoord manipulates Malachi's entire life, having foreseen his destiny as part of a grand scheme culminating on Titan.
Malachi and Beatrice are manipulated into becoming mates on Mars; they have a son and eventually find peace on Titan.
Boaz and Malachi (as 'Unk') are paired as soldier-companions on Mars; they escape together in a rocket and land on Mercury, where Boaz chooses to stay.
Chrono is Malachi's son, born on Mars; estranged from his parents, he becomes an object of veneration on Earth before vanishing with birds.
Beatrice is Chrono's mother; their relationship is distant and strange, shaped entirely by Rumfoord's manipulations.
Winston uses his foreknowledge to manipulate his own wife's life, sacrificing her happiness for his cosmic scheme.
Rumfoord and Salo become genuine friends on Titan; Rumfoord's entire scheme was unconsciously engineered by Tralfamadore to help Salo.
Salo befriends Malachi in his final years on Titan and, out of compassion, uses Tralfamadorian technology to give him a final vision of happiness.
Paul and Anita's marriage deteriorates as his disillusionment with the technocratic system grows and she clings to their status.
Ed reappears in Paul's life as a dropout from the system, encouraging Paul to question everything and eventually rebel.
Lasher recruits Paul to become the symbolic figurehead of the Ghost Shirt Society, exploiting his prestige as a disaffected engineer-hero.
The Shah tours Paul's automated world and cuts through its ideology with blunt questions that expose the hollowness Paul has begun to feel himself.
Finnerty and Lasher are co-conspirators in the Ghost Shirt Society, united by their rejection of the machine-run society.
Felix's firearms obsession gives Rudy access to the guns; the accidental shooting defines both their lives and legacies.
Kilgore Trout briefly appears in Midland City as part of the background of Rudy's world.
Walter and Mary Kathleen were college sweethearts separated by class and circumstance; he discovers she is secretly the world's most powerful businesswoman.
Ruth was Walter's devoted wife, a Holocaust survivor who died while he was imprisoned for Watergate; her memory and moral example haunt him throughout.
Walter named Leland as a Communist before HUAC, destroying his career; they meet again years later as both men try to reconstruct their ruined lives.
Trout makes a brief appearance as a fellow inmate or background figure in Walter Starbuck's post-prison world.
Leon's ghost narrates Mary's story across a million years; she becomes the unwitting matriarch of humanity's next form.
Leon witnesses Zenji's death in the Ecuadorian chaos and understands it as one of the big-brained accidents that the new evolution will eventually leave behind.
Leon narrates James Wait's life and death aboard the Bahía de Darwin, seeing his con-man existence as a tragicomic symptom of the oversized human brain.
Mary and Hisako become the core of the island's small colony of women; Hisako's furry daughter Akiko represents the evolutionary future Mary's biology lessons helped create.
Mary secretly uses the Captain's sperm to inseminate herself and the other stranded women, making him the unwitting progenitor of the entire future human species.
Wait dies at sea while briefly married to Mary; his death marks the end of his predatory old-world life and the beginning of the survivors' isolation.
Zenji and Hisako are a devoted couple; his death before reaching the Galápagos leaves Hisako to face the island's strange future alone and pregnant.
Kilgore Trout's ghost briefly appears to Leon, his son, offering him a chance to pass through the blue tunnel to the afterlife.
Together Wilbur and Eliza are a secret genius — apart they are each merely ordinary; their separation by well-meaning adults dooms both and haunts Wilbur for the rest of his life.
Circe moves into Rabo's estate and, through a mix of provocation and genuine curiosity, compels him to write his memoirs and ultimately reveal the massive secret painting in his barn.
Dan was Rabo's first master — Rabo idolized him and worked as his assistant, learning technical brilliance before Gregory's fascism and cruelty drove them apart.
Terry was Rabo's closest friend in the Abstract Expressionist movement; his accidental death with a paint sprayer haunted Rabo and shaped his retreat from the art world.
Both men are part of the New York art world that shaped Rabo, though from different eras and with opposing aesthetics — Gregory the reactionary master, Kitchen the avant-garde rebel.
After the timequake's rerun ends and free will resumes, Trout rallies people including Monica with his cry 'You were sick, but now you're well.'
Vonnegut writes himself into Timequake as Trout's nephew-like companion, blurring the boundary between author and creation.
